planting.note: Plant container stock in spring in well-drained, rocky, lean, slightly alkaline to neutral soil in full sun to part shade; bullgrass is native to rocky limestone canyons, slopes, and dry woodlands of the Chihuahuan Desert region and is highly adapted to hot, dry, alkaline conditions — it is one of the best native grasses for rocky, well-drained sites in the Texas Hill Country and western Texas.

directions: Plant in well-drained, rocky to sandy, lean, slightly alkaline to neutral soil in full sun; once established, bullgrass requires no supplemental irrigation. Muhlenbergia emersleyi (bullgrass or bull muhly) is a large, robust, clump-forming native grass of the Chihuahuan Desert and trans-Pecos Texas — one of the largest muhly grasses, producing dense, fountain-like clumps of coarse, flat, dark-green leaves 18–36 inches long and towering, feathery, purple-tinged flower plumes 4–6 feet tall in late summer and fall. While the related Gulf muhly (M. capillaris) is more widely known for its cloud of fine-textured, pink-purple fall plumes, bullgrass is larger-growing and adapted to drier, more alkaline, rocky conditions of the Trans-Pecos and Edwards Plateau. The large, showy plumes are effective in the autumn landscape for 4–6 weeks. The clumps are long-lived and provide year-round structural interest with their bold, arching leaf texture. Like all muhly grasses, bullgrass requires excellent drainage and lean soil — rich, wet soil causes it to flop and rot. An outstanding grass for hot, dry, rocky, western Texas-style landscapes.
sources: Texas A&M AgriLife Extension — Ornamental Grasses

planting.note: Plant container stock in spring in well-drained, moderately moist, slightly acidic to neutral, moderately fertile soil in full sun to part shade; Carolina laurelcherry is native to the coastal plain from North Carolina through Florida and Texas and is one of the most widely used native evergreen screening plants for Gulf Coast gardens — extremely fast-growing and adaptable.

directions: Plant in well-drained, moderately moist, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to part shade; Carolina laurelcherry is fast-growing and can be pruned as a formal hedge or allowed to grow as a multi-stemmed small tree. Prunus caroliniana (Carolina laurelcherry or cherry laurel) is one of the most useful and fastest-growing native evergreen plants for Gulf Coast privacy screens, hedges, and windbreaks — a glossy-leaved, fast-growing shrub or small tree native to the coastal plain from North Carolina through Florida and Texas, producing small clusters of fragrant white flowers in spring followed by small, black, cherry-like fruits (the 'mockingbird berry' that Northern Mockingbirds consume voraciously). The dense, lustrous, dark-green, lanceolate leaves give it a formal, refined appearance well-suited to clipped hedges and screens; it grows 2–3 feet per year when young and can form a dense privacy screen within 3–4 years. CAUTION: The leaves, seeds, and stems contain amygdalin (prussic acid precursor) and are toxic if ingested in quantity — the mature black fruits are consumed safely by birds but should not be ingested by people. Carolina laurelcherry also volunteers prolifically from bird-dispersed seeds in the Gulf South; it can become weedy in natural areas near plantings.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Native Shrubs
North Carolina State Extension — Native Trees

common_name: Mexican Lilac (Quick Stick, Mother of Cocoa)

planting.note: Plant container stock or large hardwood cuttings (which root directly in the soil) in spring in well-drained, lean to moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral, sandy to loamy soil in full sun; gliricidia is one of the world's most important multipurpose tropical trees — outstanding for living fences, shade for cocoa, and nitrogen fixation in tropical agroforestry systems.

directions: Plant container stock or large (2–4 foot) hardwood cuttings directly in the ground in spring; gliricidia establishes from large cuttings planted directly in the soil — a useful trait for creating instant living fence posts. Gliricidia sepium (Mexican lilac, quick stick, or mother of cocoa) is one of the most widely planted multipurpose tropical trees in the world — a Central American legume tree grown throughout the humid tropics for nitrogen fixation, living fences, shade for cacao and coffee plantations ('mother of cocoa' in Costa Rica), green manure, and fodder. The tree has an extraordinary capacity to root from large, dormant hardwood cuttings planted directly in the ground — a common practice for creating instant living fence posts that then leaf out and grow into full trees. In spring (usually January–March when dormant), the bare branches are covered with stunning clusters of bright pink-lavender pea-like flowers resembling redbud (Cercis) — one of the showiest tropical trees in bloom. The leaves and branches are nitrogen-rich and used as green mulch in organic farming; however, the leaves are toxic to mammals (including horses and dogs) if consumed in large quantities. CAUTION: Seeds, bark, and leaves are toxic to mammals — do not plant where livestock have access.
sources: Purdue University — New Crop Resources — Gliricidia sepium
University of Hawaii Cooperative Extension — Agroforestry

planting.note: Direct sow scarified seeds in spring in well-drained, lean to moderately fertile, slightly alkaline to neutral, rocky to sandy soil in full sun; leadtree is an extremely fast-growing tropical legume tree that is important in tropical agroforestry as a nitrogen fixer and green manure crop, but is invasive in Florida and many tropical regions — check local regulations before planting.

directions: Scarify seeds and sow in spring in well-drained, lean, slightly alkaline to neutral soil in full sun; leadtree grows extremely rapidly and fixes large amounts of nitrogen. IMPORTANT: Leucaena leucocephala is listed as invasive in Florida, Hawaii, and many tropical regions — do not plant near natural areas or waterways. Leucaena leucocephala (leadtree or wild tamarind) is a Central American tropical legume tree of extraordinary importance in global tropical agriculture — ranked among the most useful trees in the world for nitrogen fixation (fixing up to 500 lbs of nitrogen per acre per year), fast biomass production for green manure, livestock fodder, fuel wood, and erosion control in tropical agroforestry systems across Africa, Asia, and Latin America. It grows extraordinarily fast (to 15–20 feet in a year) in poor, dry soils and is highly drought-tolerant once established. Young leaves, seeds, and pods are edible as a vegetable in some cuisines; however, a toxic alkaloid (mimosine) limits consumption quantities. The tree is listed as invasive in Florida and Hawaii where it crowds out native vegetation in disturbed areas. In tropical agricultural systems, its nitrogen-fixing capacity and drought tolerance make it a key component of alley cropping systems and organic farming in the humid tropics.
sources: Purdue University — New Crop Resources — Leucaena
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Invasive Trees
